<!-- DO NOT EDIT — raw design synthesis; the clean spec supersedes this provenance artifact -->

I'll produce the design doc directly. The inputs are comprehensive — 7 domain catalogs, 3 research reports, an adversarial critique, and a stress-test matrix. My job is to adjudicate the genuine conflict (open-vs-closed topology) and produce a section-by-section spec the architect approves.

The single sharpest tension: the domain catalogs argue for an OPEN trait, the adversary argues decisively for CLOSED. But the owner has already confirmed the topology becomes OPEN/extensible. So I honor that decision and absorb the adversary's strongest objections as *hard constraints on how open works* — framework owns instance storage, revoke-fence is enforced not delegated, admission is acquire-and-park not read-and-decide. That reframing dissolves most of the conflict.

Here is the design.

---

# Spec 1 — Open, Engine-Managed Resource Topology

**Status:** brainstorm-ready. **Layer:** Business (`nebula-resource`). **Consumes:** existing `Resource` trait (Config/Runtime), `AnyManagedResource` erasure, `SlotIdentity` dedup, eventbus. **Produces:** the availability seam Spec 2 (engine resource-aware scheduling) reads.

---

## 1. Architecture overview

A **Resource** is the author-implemented managed client (`create`/`check`/`shutdown`/`destroy`/`on_credential_*`, two assoc types `Config`/`Runtime`) — already shipped, unchanged. A **Topology** is the engine-facing lifecycle contract describing how instances of that resource are *leased*: it is becoming an **open trait** an author may implement in a plugin crate, with a closed set of audited batteries-included impls. The critical structural rule that makes "open" safe: **the Manager owns instance storage; a Topology owns only the lease/permit policy over storage the Manager hands it.** A Topology never holds its own connection cache, `static` map, or `Arc<Pool>` — that is what re-opens cross-tenant bleed (adversary §3), so it is forbidden by API shape, not by discipline.

```
author crate                          nebula-resource (framework-owned)                      engine (Spec 2)
┌─────────────┐   impl Resource    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Resource   │ ─────────────────► │  Manager                                     │
│  (Config,   │   impl Topology    │   • registry row = (Key, Scope, SlotIdentity)│
│   Runtime)  │ ─────────(opt)───► │   • dedup / scope lookup        [UNIFORM]    │
└─────────────┘                    │   • revoke-epoch fence          [UNIFORM]    │
                                   │   • rotation fan-out            [UNIFORM]    │
        ▲                          │   • recovery-gate + reaper      [UNIFORM]    │
        │ Topology trait           │   • drain / quiesce             [UNIFORM]    │
        │ (acquire policy,         │   • metrics / events / spans    [UNIFORM]    │
        │  availability surface,   │   • InstanceStore (framework-held)[UNIFORM]  │
        │  reset/destroy hooks)    │                                              │
        │                          │   ┌────────── per-Topology ─────────────┐   │
        └──────────────────────────┼──►│ try_reserve → Ticket  (concurrency  │   │
                                   │   │ acquire(Ticket,&store)→ Guard  gate) │   │
                                   │   │ on_release(&mut Runtime) reset       │   │
                                   │   │ availability() → AdmitSnapshot       │   │
                                   │   └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │
                                   │                                              │
                                   │   erased acquire: Arc<dyn Fn(..) ->          │
                                   │     BoxFuture<Result<ResourceGuard>>>        │
                                   └────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
                                                        │  ResourceGuard (deref Runtime,
                                                        │   RAII release → on_release → store)
                                                        ▼
                                            ┌───────────────────────────┐
                                            │ admission surface (read)  │◄── Spec 2 reads:
                                            │  phase() : Copy snapshot   │     try_reserve / phase /
                                            │  load()  : Option<Metric>  │     load (advisory)
                                            └───────────────────────────┘
```

**Uniform-in-Manager (one audited implementation, never author-touchable):** dedup by `(Key, Scope, SlotIdentity)+fingerprint`; scope hierarchy resolution; the **revoke-epoch fence** (HikariCP-#1836: snapshot epoch at create-start, re-check under lock on every return-to-storage path, destroy-not-recycle on stale); rotation fan-out (`on_credential_refresh`/`on_credential_revoke` walked across all live instances of a row); recovery-gate + maintenance reaper; two-phase drain/quiesce; instance storage; metrics/spans/events.

**Per-Topology (author writes, small):** the concurrency gate (`try_reserve` → ticket), how a ticket becomes a leased `Runtime` over framework-held storage (`acquire`), the release-time reset (`on_release`), and the read-only availability snapshot (`availability`).

This split is the whole design: authors express *lease policy*, the framework keeps *every credential/tenant/durability invariant*.

---

## 2. The open Topology contract (the core)

### 2.1 The two traits

Authoring uses an RPITIT trait (ergonomic, no `async_trait`); the Manager consumes a boxed-future erasure of it (object-safe), exactly mirroring the `Resource`/`AnyManagedResource` split already shipped. Authors implement the RPITIT trait; the framework's blanket impl produces the erased form. **Authors never see `Box<dyn Any>`** — the erasure boundary is closed by making the Topology generic over the resource's `Runtime`, not over `Any`.

```rust
/// Author-facing. Generic over the resource's Runtime — the guard type is
/// concrete to the author, so the Any-downcast the adversary flagged never
/// reaches author code. `forbid(unsafe_code)`, thiserror errors.
pub trait Topology: Send + Sync + 'static {
    /// The leasable unit the framework stores. For Pooled this is one connection;
    /// for Resident it is the shared handle; for Bounded it is `()` (permit-only).
    type Slot: Send + Sync;

    // ---- concurrency gate: admission is a value, not a bool (resolves TOCTOU) ----

    /// Non-blocking. Returns a permit-bearing Ticket or a typed Unavailable.
    /// The Ticket IS the reservation — holding it guarantees a slot; dropping it
    /// releases it. There is no separate "is there room?" boolean to lie.
    fn try_reserve(&self, store: &InstanceStore<Self::Slot>)
        -> Result<Ticket<Self::Slot>, Unavailable>;

    /// Async, fallible. Consumes a Ticket; produces the live slot to lease.
    /// May do network I/O (channel-open, page-open, post-checkout SET) — this is
    /// the per-acquire session-init hook (A4). The framework wraps the result in
    /// a ResourceGuard whose Drop runs `on_release` then returns the slot.
    fn acquire(&self, ticket: Ticket<Self::Slot>, store: &InstanceStore<Self::Slot>)
        -> impl Future<Output = Result<Lease<Self::Slot>, Error>> + Send;

    /// Async, fallible, ordered-before-reissue. Reset slot to a clean baseline
    /// (rollback txn, reset PRAGMAs, UNSUBSCRIBE, cancel transient consumers).
    /// Err  => framework EVICTS the slot (destroy, do not return to store).
    /// Ok   => framework returns slot to store for the next acquirer.
    /// On panic-in-guard the framework treats the slot as POISONED => evict (A5).
    fn on_release(&self, slot: &mut Self::Slot)
        -> impl Future<Output = Result<(), Error>> + Send { async { Ok(()) } }

    // ---- availability surface (sync, Copy, advisory — Section 3) ----

    fn phase(&self, store: &InstanceStore<Self::Slot>) -> AdmissionPhase;
    fn load(&self, store: &InstanceStore<Self::Slot>) -> Option<Load> { None }
}
```

**Why `Ticket` (not a boolean) resolves check-then-acquire TOCTOU.** The tower `poll_ready` lesson (issues #408/#412/#431) and tokio #898 both prove: a readiness *boolean* is a lie the instant after it returns, and a readiness *reservation held in `&mut self`* gets stranded. The fix both projects converged on — return a **droppable capability token** — is what `try_reserve` does. The ticket carries an `OwnedSemaphorePermit` (Bounded/Pooled) or is zero-cost (Resident). The engine's admission decision *is* `try_reserve`: success means a slot is genuinely held; failure returns a typed `Unavailable { retry_after }` to park on. There is no second, weaker signal to race against. `acquire` consumes the ticket, so you cannot acquire without first reserving, and you cannot reserve without holding capacity.

```rust
pub enum Unavailable {
    Saturated { retry_after: Option<Duration> }, // capacity full now — park & reschedule
    Warming,                                      // instance not query-ready yet (index build, cold start)
    Recovering,                                   // mid-reconnect / mid-reset
    Tainted,                                       // creds revoked / poisoned / unrecoverable-until-recovery-gate
}
```

### 2.2 ResourceGuard and release semantics

`acquire` yields a `Lease<Slot>`; the Manager wraps it in a `ResourceGuard` that `Deref`s to the resource's `Runtime`. On guard drop the framework runs the release path **as real async work, scheduled on a release task — not in `Drop`** (canon §11.4 is revised here for server-side-stateful resources, see §5): `on_release(slot)` runs; `Ok` returns the slot to `InstanceStore` *under the revoke-epoch fence*; `Err` or panic evicts and destroys. The guard never lets an author re-pool a dirty or credential-stale slot — that decision is the Manager's, keyed on the fence, not the author's `on_release`.

### 2.3 A custom author topology in ~30 lines (FFmpeg pool, case #7)

```rust
pub struct FfmpegPool { sem: Arc<Semaphore> }  // permit-only; process spawned in action body

impl Topology for FfmpegPool {
    type Slot = ();                              // null/permit-only Runtime (A5 / case #7)

    fn try_reserve(&self, _s: &InstanceStore<()>) -> Result<Ticket<()>, Unavailable> {
        match self.sem.clone().try_acquire_owned() {
            Ok(permit) => Ok(Ticket::permit(permit)),       // the permit IS the reservation
            Err(_)     => Err(Unavailable::Saturated { retry_after: None }),
        }
    }

    async fn acquire(&self, ticket: Ticket<()>, _s: &InstanceStore<()>) -> Result<Lease<()>, Error> {
        Ok(Lease::from_permit(ticket))               // hand back the permit-guard; action spawns ffmpeg
    }
    // on_release: default (permit drop frees capacity) — nothing to reset

    fn phase(&self, _s: &InstanceStore<()>) -> AdmissionPhase {
        if self.sem.available_permits() == 0 { AdmissionPhase::Saturated } else { AdmissionPhase::Ready }
    }
    fn load(&self, _s: &InstanceStore<()>) -> Option<Load> {
        Some(Load::permits(self.sem.available_permits(), self.cap))   // used/total, advisory
    }
}
```

For free, because the Manager owns everything else, this FFmpeg pool gets: dedup by `(binary-path+hwaccel-fp, scope, SlotIdentity)`; rotation fan-out if it ever grows a credential slot; recovery-gate + reaper; two-phase drain on shutdown; acquire/wait tracing spans; saturation/recovery `ResourceEvent`s; and the engine admission read. The author wrote lease policy and a load gauge; the framework supplied the moat.

### 2.4 Built-ins that ship

Closed, audited, framework-owned — these carry the revoke-fence proof so authors rarely implement from scratch:

- **Pooled** — N interchangeable, checkout/recycle. `Slot = the connection`. `on_release` runs reset-before-repool (A5 applies to Pooled, not just Exclusive). Cases #1, #5(R5), #19-channels.
- **Resident** — one shared handle, clone-on-acquire, `on_release` no-op. `try_reserve` is infallible (unbounded logical concurrency). Cases #2, #9, #14, #18, #21, most cloud SDKs.
- **Bounded (RESTORED)** — runtime-configurable cap, three modes:
  - `Capped(n)` where `n` is read from config/JSON at registration (NOT const generic), validated as a typed `Error` at registration, never a panic — mirrors `PoolRuntime::try_new` fail-closed. Cases #7, #22-license, permit governors.
  - `Exclusive` (`n == 1` with reset-ordering + poison-on-panic). Cases #11 serial-port, #22-device.
  - `Unbounded` (token-gated, no concurrency cap; backpressure is logical-inflight). Degenerate Resident-with-a-gauge.

  Bounded's cap is **runtime-set-and-queryable** because the unanimous evidence (license seats, `num_cpus`, MIG slices, peer-negotiated `MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS`, broker channel-max) is that *no resource in any catalog has a compile-time-fixed N*. The cap may even change at runtime (license shrink, MIG reconfig) — `Bounded` exposes `set_cap(n)` that grows/shrinks the semaphore via `add_permits`/`forget_permits`.

**Adversary concession absorbed:** the open trait does **not** reuse the sealed `AnyManagedResource`. It introduces a *separate* object-safe `ErasedTopology` (boxed futures), and the revoke/rotation/drain invariants stay in Manager code operating on `InstanceStore`, never delegated to author code. An author topology *cannot* hold instances, so it *cannot* bypass `SlotIdentity` — the structural barrier the adversary defends is preserved by the `InstanceStore`-not-author-storage rule (Constraint in §7).

---

## 3. The availability / admission surface (for Spec 2)

> **This is the seam Spec 2 (engine resource-aware scheduling) consumes. Spec 1 designs the hooks; Spec 1 does NOT design the scheduler.**

Three layers, ordered cheap→expensive, deliberately minimal and TOCTOU-honest.

### 3.1 The authoritative layer: `try_reserve` (gate = grant)

The **only** signal the engine may *gate* on is `try_reserve`, because it is the only one that cannot lie: success hands back a held `Ticket`. The scheduler's admission loop is **try-reserve-and-park, never read-and-decide** (adversary §2 — `stats()` is documented non-atomic; any pre-acquire gauge is TOCTOU-by-construction). Spec 2 dispatch:

```
on step needing resource R:
    match topology.try_reserve(&store):
        Ok(ticket)                          => dispatch worker; worker calls acquire(ticket)
        Err(Saturated { retry_after })      => park step, reschedule after retry_after (priority-weighted)
        Err(Warming | Recovering)           => park step, reschedule on next phase change event
        Err(Tainted)                        => fail step or route to recovery-gate
```

### 3.2 The advisory layer: `phase()` — cheap cached `Copy` snapshot

```rust
#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq)]
pub enum AdmissionPhase { Ready, Warming, Recovering, Saturated, Tainted }
```

This is an **orthogonal admission axis**, NOT folded into the shipped lifecycle `ResourcePhase` (Initializing/Ready/Reloading/Draining/ShuttingDown/Failed). The catalogs proved 12 cases need `Warming` (index build, cold start, model load — distinct from "Initializing = being constructed"), `Recovering` (mid-reconnect/rebalance), `Tainted` (post-failover suspect / creds revoked). `phase()` is for **prioritization and UI** (which resources to *prefer*, what to show operators), updated by the periodic active check (§3.3). **`Saturated`-as-phase is advisory only** — the adversary is right that gating on it is a TOCTOU lie, so the *gate* is always `try_reserve`; `phase()==Saturated` only tells the scheduler "don't bother try_reserving this one first." This split satisfies both the 12 domain cases and the adversary.

### 3.3 The active layer: `check()` scheduling contract + `load()`

Active health stays on the existing `Resource::check` (author-side) + `RecoveryGate`. Spec 1's addition is a **cost class** so Spec 2 schedules expensive probes less often (case #1 `SELECT 1` is cheap, #6 `PRAGMA quick_check` / #9 `DescribeTable` are not; #21 health-check consumes quota):

```rust
pub enum CheckCost { Cheap, Moderate, Expensive }   // engine: Cheap ~10s, Expensive ~minutes
fn check_cost(&self) -> CheckCost { CheckCost::Cheap }   // on Resource, default cheap
```

`load()` returns an **optional, author-supplied, generic snapshot** — never a fixed `(open,max)`. The metric shape varies in every catalog (pool occupancy, inflight-pipeline depth, CMAP wait-queue, server `max_concurrent_queries`, PEL/lag, RCU/WCU headroom, VRAM, byte-budget), so it is one `PartialOrd` scalar plus an opaque detail, modeled on tower `Load`:

```rust
#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
pub struct Load {
    pub saturation: f32,            // 0.0..=1.0, PartialOrd — least-loaded routing in Spec 2
    pub est_wait: Option<Duration>, // None when meaningless (FFmpeg variance, case #7)
    pub detail: LoadDetail,         // Permits{used,total} | Inflight(u32) | Lag(u64) | ByteBudget{used,max} | None
}
```

`load()` is **diagnostics + routing-preference only, never a gate** (adversary §4 — exposing `available_permits` to the scheduler welds it to topology internals). Spec 2 may use `saturation` to *rank* candidates (p2c-style) among those that *already returned `Ok` from `try_reserve`*; it may not use it to decide admission. `None` is a first-class answer (cases #2, #3 — shared singletons have no honest local load).

**Boundary stated explicitly:** rate-limit/token-budget (cases #3, #5, #21 temporal budgets) is **enforced in `nebula-resilience`, surfaced read-only here**. The limiter *drives* `phase()` (sets `Saturated`/`Recovering` from 429/Retry-After headers) and may populate `load().saturation`; the topology owns no rate accounting. This keeps the R8-governor trap closed: temporal budgets refill on a clock, not on release, so they are never `Bounded` permits.

---

## 4. DX walkthrough

### 4.1 Minimal case — one shared HTTP client (case #2), 11 lines

```rust
#[derive(Resource)]
#[resource(topology = Resident)]              // the entire topology choice: one attribute
struct HttpClient { client: reqwest::Client }

impl Resource for HttpClient {
    type Config = HttpConfig;
    type Runtime = reqwest::Client;
    async fn create(&self, cfg: &HttpConfig) -> Result<Self::Runtime, Error> {
        Ok(reqwest::Client::builder().timeout(cfg.timeout).build()?)
    }
    // check/shutdown/destroy: derive defaults. No topology code at all.
}
```

The author writes **zero topology code** and gets dedup, rotation, recovery, drain, metrics, and a `phase()==Ready` admission read. `load()` defaults to `None` (honest — reqwest's pool is not introspectable).

### 4.2 Exotic case — gRPC N-streams (case #9), author ships a custom topology

The cap is *peer-negotiated* (`MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS`, mutates mid-connection), so `load()` must pull-from-live-resource, not a stored const — the one thing the closed enum can't pre-name. The author still writes only the lease policy:

```rust
struct GrpcStreams { channel: Channel }       // Slot = the shared HTTP/2 channel

impl Topology for GrpcStreams {
    type Slot = Channel;
    fn try_reserve(&self, s: &InstanceStore<Channel>) -> Result<Ticket<Channel>, Unavailable> {
        match self.channel.connectivity_state() {            // pull live, not cached
            Ready    => Ok(Ticket::shared(s.shared())),       // clone-on-acquire, stream is the real unit
            Idle | Connecting => Err(Unavailable::Warming),
            TransientFailure  => Err(Unavailable::Recovering),
            Shutdown          => Err(Unavailable::Tainted),
        }
    }
    async fn acquire(&self, t: Ticket<Channel>, _s: &InstanceStore<Channel>) -> Result<Lease<Channel>, Error> {
        Ok(Lease::shared(t))                                 // open the stream in the action over this channel
    }
    fn phase(&self, _s: &InstanceStore<Channel>) -> AdmissionPhase { /* map connectivity_state */ }
    fn load(&self, _s: &InstanceStore<Channel>) -> Option<Load> {
        let used = self.channel.active_streams();
        let max  = self.channel.max_concurrent_streams();    // peer-negotiated, read live
        Some(Load { saturation: used as f32 / max as f32, est_wait: None, detail: Inflight(used) })
    }
}
```

**Line/concept comparison:**

| Model | Minimal HTTP client | Exotic (gRPC streams / FFmpeg) | Concepts an author must learn |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dagster** `ConfigurableResource` | ~6 lines | not expressible (no topology taxonomy — pooling is hand-rolled per author, no engine admission) | typed object + setup/teardown |
| **Current nebula** (closed `{Pooled, Resident}`) | 1 attribute | **impossible** — gRPC-stream / FFmpeg-pool have no variant; author abuses `Resident` with a load-correctness hole | topology enum |
| **This spec** | 1 attribute, 0 topology code | ~20 lines, no friction, full admission + moat for free | `Resource` + (optional) `Topology` trait |

Nebula matches Dagster's minimal-case ergonomics (one attribute) while delivering what Dagster structurally cannot: an engine that knows and manages the lifecycle (the moat). The exotic case costs ~20 author lines versus *impossible* in both Dagster and current nebula.

---

## 5. Capability scope: 1.0 / 1.1 / CUT

Driven by the stress-test's contract-additions list (A1–A14) and the adversary's cut list.

### 1.0 MUST-HAVE

| Item | Why (one line) | Forced by |
|---|---|---|
| **A1 Bounded restored, runtime cap, typed-Error validation** | The one undisputed gap; unanimous across every domain *and* the adversary. | #7,#11,#22 |
| **A14 Open Topology trait (framework-storage-only)** | Owner-confirmed; gRPC/SSH/browser/disposable shapes can't be pre-named. Made safe by InstanceStore rule. | #4,#8,#10,#17,R4-DB,R6 |
| **A2 Admission phase axis** (`Warming`/`Recovering`/`Tainted`) | 12 cases; distinct from lifecycle `ResourcePhase`; `Initializing≠Warming`. | 12 cases |
| **Ticket-based `try_reserve`** (resolves TOCTOU) | The admission signal must not be a lie; gate = grant. | tower #412, adversary §2 |
| **A3 Optional `load()`, diagnostics+routing only** | Admission = try-reserve-and-park, never read-and-decide. | adversary §2/§4 + 12 cases |
| **A4 Per-acquire session-init + A5 fallible async reset-on-release + A6 graceful destroy(timeout)** | Correctness bug, not nicety — session state leaks across tenants without it; missed teardown leaks server-side quota. Revises canon §11.4. | #1,#8,#11,#17,#19,#20 |
| **A10 Dedup affinity/anti-share modes** (share / affinity-key / anti-dedup), framework-enforced | Stateful sessions must NOT share same config; disposables never share; framework-enforced so author caches can't reopen bleed. | #6,#11,#16,#17 |
| **A11 Cost-aware semantic `check()`** | Socket-liveness is a false-green for every multiplexed/streaming shape. | 7 cases |
| **A12 Parent-generation recovery** | Conn death must atomically invalidate the channel pool. | #8,#17,#19,#20 |

### 1.1 DEFER

| Item | Why deferred |
|---|---|
| **A7 generic renew-while-held + fencing token** | Scope only the lock-lease family; generalize after a real second consumer. Genuinely outside acquire→guard→release. |
| **A8 suspend/passivation across durable wait** | Biggest structural break, but durability belongs to the **engine/storage tier** (journal), not the topology trait. 1.0 only ensures the guard *can* detach and carries a reattach key. Templates: Trigger.dev onWait/onResume, DBOS config_name registry. |
| **A9 streaming handles** | Possibly engine-daemon land (retired EventSource shape). 1.0 only "does not forbid" a guard outliving one call. |
| **A13 first-class supervised background task** | Fits-by-convention today (JoinHandle in Runtime, abort in shutdown); low case-count. |
| Built-in `Multiplexed` / `ExclusiveStatefulOwner` / `Ephemeral` batteries | Ship as audited built-ins **iff** their integrations are 1.0 targets; else authors use the open trait in the interim. |

### CUT as speculative (heed the adversary)

| Item | Why cut |
|---|---|
| **`Saturated`-as-a-gating-phase** | Load dressed as phase; TOCTOU. Kept as *advisory* `phase()` value only; the gate is `try_reserve`. |
| **Engine-scheduled health *poller* as new surface** | `Resource::check` + RecoveryGate + reaper already exist; no consumer for a new poller. |
| **Graded load as an admission *input*** | Demoted to diagnostics-only; never feeds the scheduler's gate. |
| **Lease-renewal as a general contract (1.0)** | No second consumer; invites half-renewed leases racing revoke. (The lock-family slice is A7-deferred, not cut.) |

---

## 6. Decomposition + migration

### Spec dependency order

```
Spec 1 (THIS): open Topology contract + restored Bounded + admission surface
   │  produces: try_reserve(Ticket) / phase() / load() / check_cost()  ← the seam
   ▼
Spec 2: engine resource-aware scheduling  (consumes the seam; try-reserve-and-park,
   │                                        priority-weighted park queue, p2c routing on load())
   ▼
Deferred items 5–8 from the prior plan (independent of Spec 2, can interleave):
   5. typed Registration (replace stringly config with typed builder)
   6. error seam (topology errors → engine backpressure taxonomy)
   7. surface amputation (delete the legacy phase-only projection once admission axis lands)
   8. (A8) durable passivation — engine/storage tier, needs Spec 2's park model first
```

Spec 2 depends on Spec 1's seam. Deferred 5–7 are local to `nebula-resource` and can land alongside Spec 1. Deferred 8 (durable suspend) depends on Spec 2's park/reschedule model.

### What changes vs the 4 landed commits

- **Restore `Bounded`** as a built-in `Topology` impl (it was deleted). Runtime cap, three modes, `try_new`-style fail-closed validation. This is part of Spec 1.
- **Promote `Topology` from closed enum to open trait** + introduce `ErasedTopology` (boxed-future object-safe form) alongside the existing `AnyManagedResource` (do **not** widen the sealed trait — the adversary's semver-seal argument holds; topology gets its own erasure).
- **Add `InstanceStore<Slot>`** as the framework-owned storage handle topologies borrow but never own. The existing `PoolRuntime`/`ResidentRuntime` internal fields become `InstanceStore` specializations; the 2-arm `match` in `bump_revoke_epoch`/`dispatch_slot_hook` becomes a uniform fence over `InstanceStore` (the per-topology no-op cases collapse because storage is uniform).
- **Add the admission axis**: `AdmissionPhase` (new enum, orthogonal to `ResourcePhase`), `Load`, `CheckCost`, `Ticket`/`Unavailable`. The legacy phase-only engine projection stays until Spec 2 lands the admission read, then is amputated (deferred #7).
- **No shims.** Hard break: `Topology` consumers re-dispatch through the trait. Pre-1.0, this is acceptable per the constraints.

---

## 7. Risks + open questions

### Risks

1. **Erasure leak onto authors (mitigated, not eliminated).** The RPITIT-authored `Topology` is generic over `Slot`, so authors never write `Box<dyn Any>` or `Pin<Box<dyn Future>>` — the framework's blanket impl erases. Residual risk: the `Lease<Slot>`/`Ticket<Slot>` types must be ergonomic enough that authors don't reach around them. Mitigation: ship the three batteries as worked examples; gate on a derive-macro path for the common cases.

2. **TOCTOU (resolved by construction).** `try_reserve→Ticket→acquire(ticket)` makes the admission grant a held value; you cannot acquire without a ticket, cannot ticket without capacity. The only gauge (`load()`) is explicitly non-gating. This is the tower #412 / tokio #898 fix applied wholesale.

3. **Scheduler coupling to topology internals (mitigated by the closed vocabulary).** Spec 2 couples only to `Ticket`/`Unavailable` (closed enum) + `AdmissionPhase` (closed) + `Load.saturation` (a single `f32`, opaque `detail` ignored by the scheduler). It never reads `available_permits`/`open`/`max`. A new topology with no semaphore still returns a `saturation` and a `Ticket` — no ripple into engine code or the deny.toml-gated api projection.

4. **Author implements topology wrong → cross-tenant bleed (mitigated structurally).** The adversary's sharpest attack. **Mitigation is the `InstanceStore` rule, enforced by API shape:** a `Topology` has no field for instance storage and is handed `&InstanceStore<Slot>` it cannot retain past a call (lifetime-bound, not `'static`). It physically cannot build a host-keyed `static` cache that bypasses `SlotIdentity`, because it never owns instances. The revoke-epoch fence runs in Manager code on every return-to-store path regardless of what the author's `on_release` did. An author can write a *slow* or *incorrect-reset* topology (correctness of their own resource), but cannot reopen the tenant barrier or skip the revoke fence — those live above the seam.

5. **Reset/destroy now async + fallible (revises canon §11.4).** Moving release off `Drop` onto a release task means a crashed worker can still skip teardown. Mitigation: the framework's drain/reaper reconciles orphaned slots (poison-on-missing-release → evict); cost/safety-critical guaranteed-destroy (case #6 money, #11/#22 hardware) is explicitly **out-of-scope for the in-process manager** and flagged for an external janitor in a later spec.

### Open questions for the architect

1. **Which batteries are 1.0 vs 1.1?** Bounded is 1.0-confirmed. Are `Multiplexed` (SSH/AMQP-channel/browser-page) and `ExclusiveStatefulOwner` (Kafka-consumer) 1.0 built-ins, or do their integrations slip to 1.1 (authors use the open trait meanwhile)? This gates how much audited code lands now.

2. **Does `load().saturation` feed Spec 2 routing, or is even that too much coupling?** I recommend yes (p2c over a single `f32` among already-reserved candidates is the tower-validated minimal coupling). The architect may prefer Spec 2 do zero load-based routing in 1.0 (admit-or-park only), leaving `load()` purely diagnostic. Decide before Spec 2 starts.

3. **Where does the A8 durable-passivation reattach-key live — `nebula-resource` or the journal?** I recommend the journal (engine/storage tier owns durability; resource only exposes a `reattach_key()` and a detachable guard). Confirm the tier boundary now so Spec 1's guard type reserves the detach hook without implementing it.

---

**Net:** topology becomes an **open trait** (owner-confirmed) made safe by the **framework-owns-storage** rule that defuses the adversary's bleed/erasure/revoke objections; the admission seam is **ticket-based try-reserve** (gate) + advisory `phase()`/`load()` (route/diagnose), resolving TOCTOU by construction; **Bounded is restored** with a runtime cap; durability/streaming/renewal are deferred to the engine tier or 1.1. The contract additions cluster around one truth the catalogs forced: **`release`/`destroy`/`check` are real async work, not `Drop` glue**, and **admission is an orthogonal axis fed by try-reserve outcomes, not a gauge**.
